InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 5

Phase 1, Delivered

The retrospective record of four weeks of structured UX development on InnerSeed Garden — what was done, what it produced, and what it changed. This document closes the dark chapter: Phase 1 lived in the roots, and everything that follows emerges into the light.

DocumentWeek 5 Retrospective Period covered4 – 27 June 2026 StatusPhase 1 complete & sealed Prepared forInnerSeed Developers
Phase 1 is complete. The full desktop UX audit was delivered across four weekly manifestos, closing with a formal review on 27 June 2026. This retrospective consolidates the achievement record. The four weekly manifestos remain the master evidence base in Drive; nothing here reopens them.
How this document reports. Each week is presented through a simple evaluation logic drawn from professional communications measurement practice (the AMEC Integrated Evaluation Framework): Activity — what was done; Output — what it produced; Outcome — what changed as a result. This keeps the record honest: achievements are claimed only where a traceable output or decision exists.
01

Phase 1 by the numbers

49Structured findings
D-01 → D-49
5Week 1 carry-overs
W1-01 → W1-05
4Weekly manifestos
delivered & filed
4Focus calls
logged & actioned
5Surfaces confirmed for the consolidated language ticket (D-03)
1Standout commercial discovery — Spotify import (D-43)
2Full structural proposals — stacked Builder & share card
1Independent external UX review secured at no cost

Behind the numbers sits a consistent working method: live hands-on testing, raw observations logged verbatim, findings structured into a running D-xx log with type, priority and cross-references, and every recommendation grounded in either observed user behaviour or established usability theory. The audit did not just collect problems — it connected them, escalating isolated symptoms into consolidated development tickets and structural recommendations.

02

The record, week by week

Week 1 · 4 June

First exploration — the foundations of the method

Activity

First hands-on session covering the Session Builder, preset playback, transport controls and post-session navigation, approached deliberately as a first-time user.

Output

Five foundational findings (W1-01 to W1-05) and two original design concepts: the pause-intercept kindness message and the post-session landing screen.

Outcome

The audit method was established — structured findings with priorities and recommendations — and the hidden mode switcher (W1-01) was flagged as the first major discoverability issue, a thread that ran through the entire phase.

Week 2 · 10–13 June

The five-question audit — language, hierarchy and the invisible nav

Activity

A structured five-question audit of the opening screen, plus live testing of Preset mode, session cards, navigation and the dashboard journey, with observations from showing the app to others.

Output

The D-xx findings log launched. Key discoveries: "Presets" terminology observed causing live disengagement (D-01), the hover-only navigation invisible to new users (D-04), the missing discovery layer (D-05), and the first sighting of the critical Dutch-language instability (D-03).

Outcome

A defining insight for the whole audit: the app rewards users who already know what they want. The strategic question shifted from "what do you want to build?" to "here's what this can do for you" — a reframing that shaped every recommendation that followed.

Where the audit began — the dashboard on first load. Nav collapsed to icons, “Build your breathwork” as the dominant CTA, and the “Presets” language that became finding D-01.
Week 3 · 17–18 June

Completion flow, Green House, and theory brought to bear

Activity

Full observation of the session completion flow, first entry into Green House, deliberate re-testing of unstable surfaces, and the 18 June focus call in which Jeroen set out the Green House philosophy and the Root · Rise · Return throughline directly.

Output

The locked post-completion interface identified and escalated (D-30); the Load Session modal's content instability documented as a single underlying data issue rather than scattered symptoms (D-23); and a standalone expert design note on the settings cog, grounded in Nielsen's recognition-over-recall principle, Norman's affordance–signifier distinction and cognitive load theory.

Outcome

Green House moved from unscoped placeholder to understood, deliberate feature — a connection space in Jeroen's own words — and the feedback loop went live: findings aligned with the developer's own thinking were being implemented in the product during the audit itself.

Green House, opened and understood — “Your Rhythm,” a twelve-month activity record, and the Progress, Coaches and Events spaces that Jeroen framed as the connection dimension of Root · Rise · Return.
Week 4 · 22 June

Builder mode — where the money is

Activity

The full audit lens turned on Builder mode — the app's most distinctive and commercially significant feature — testing every pillar, every transport icon and every hidden action in turn.

Output

Eleven findings (D-39 to D-49), sixteen figures, and two complete design proposals: the full-width stacked Builder layout resolving four findings at once, and the session share card turning a completed build into a product worth sharing.

Outcome

The Spotify playlist import (D-43) confirmed as the standout commercial discovery of the phase — a premium-feeling differentiator hidden behind an unlabelled icon — and the commercial logic of Builder mode articulated: a user who shares a session acquires another user at zero cost.

The problem as found — five unlabelled transport icons performing critical functions with no indication of their purpose (D-42, D-45).
The answer as proposed — the full-width stacked Builder layout: numbered steps, labelled functions, and Spotify surfaced as a named, visible feature. One proposal resolving D-39, D-43, D-44 and D-48.
03

Headline outcomes

Four results stand above the rest of the record. Each moved something in the product, the roadmap, or the commercial thinking — the test this retrospective applies to every claimed achievement.

Commercial

The Spotify discovery (D-43). The audit surfaced a feature the product already had but the user could not find: personal Spotify playlist import into a breathwork session. No mainstream wellness competitor was identified offering an equivalent. What began as a discoverability finding became a named commercial differentiator, carried forward by name into the Phase 2 marketing strategy.

The discovery itself — the Spotify integration panel: a curated Garden Playlist and personal playlist import, found behind an unlabelled icon.
Structural

Threefold consistency (D-21). The audit's highest-priority structural recommendation: mode switching between Preset, Builder and Creator was found duplicated across disconnected, unlabelled locations, deepening with each week's testing. Rather than logging each instance separately, the audit consolidated them into a single structural case — one navigation model, labelled, in one place.

Technical

The consolidated language ticket (D-03). Dutch-language content surfacing for English users was confirmed across five separate surfaces — page level, the Load Session modal, Guided Breaths, Filter mode, and the "Find your breath" modal. The audit's contribution was diagnostic: five symptoms, one underlying cause, one development ticket. This is the difference between a bug list and an audit.

Process

A live feedback loop. Confirmed directly in the 18 June focus call: findings from the manifestos were being processed and, where they aligned with the developer's own direction, implemented in the product during the audit itself. The work was not a report waiting to be read — it was shaping the app while it was being written.

From configuration screen to product — the proposed session share card: artwork, all three components summarised, Share and Save both visible. The design output that carries Builder mode’s commercial logic.
04

How the work was done

The credibility of the findings rests on the discipline behind them. Three practices defined the phase:

Raw first, structured second. Every session began with observations logged verbatim — first impressions preserved exactly as stated — before being structured into findings. First-time-user reactions are unrepeatable evidence; the method protected them.

Theory where it earns its place. Recommendations drew on established usability research — Nielsen's heuristics, Norman's The Design of Everyday Things, cognitive load theory — deployed to explain patterns, not to decorate them. The settings cog analysis stands as the model: a small interface element read through recognition-over-recall, affordance versus signifier, and working-memory cost.

A single source of truth. The SSOT workflow — raw notes to Drive, structured in session, filed as styled HTML, rebuilt and verified at the next session — meant no finding, decision or focus-call direction was lost across four weeks and two collaborators. This document is itself a product of that system.

05

Close-out and what carries forward

Phase 1 was formally reviewed and closed on 27 June 2026, with the desktop audit acknowledged as complete. The following items transfer into Phase 2 as live, owned actions — not loose ends:

ItemStatus at closePhase 2 home
Mobile testing (Preset & Builder journeys, Filter mode)Deliberately sequenced to follow the desktop auditFirst deployment of the User Testing System
D-03 consolidated language ticket (five surfaces)Confirmed, diagnosed, single ticketDevelopment priority with Jeroen
D-21 mode-switching consolidationHighest-priority structural recommendationDevelopment discussion, informed by stacked layout proposal
D-49 Guided Voices empty selectorOpen pending voice availabilityRaised with Jeroen; interim empty-state decision required
Analytics implementationExisting implementation noted; measurement list drafted from findingsReview and map against audit findings
Independent human UX reviewCompleted externally at no costCross-referenced against the findings log

Phase 2 shifts the project from audit to build: a User Testing System owned by ISD, and the Content Marketing Strategy & Sales Development Process Manual as the flagship deliverable — with the brand's identity, voice and visuals treated as fixed inputs the strategy draws on. The founder interview in Enschede stands as agreed cornerstone content for that work.

Phase 1 lived in dark mode — the roots. This is the last document written in it. Phase 2 emerges into the app's own light: from root, to rise.
InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 5 — Phase 1 Retrospective.
Compiled from the Week 1–4 Manifestos, session logs and focus-call records held in Google Drive (master copies). Reporting logic: AMEC Integrated Evaluation Framework (Activity → Output → Outcome). Phase 1 sealed 27 June 2026. Root · Rise · Return.
